user_prompt: Explain Bernoulli’s principle using an airplane wing in depth. Use a single commercial airplane wing flying steadily through the air at cruising speed. No humans. Scene 1: establish the wing and airflow, showing smooth air approaching and splitting above and below the wing. Scene 2: zoom closer to the wing c

style_bible.visual_style: Realistic 3D rendering with clear, smooth airflow visualization using semi-transparent colored streams and particles
style_bible.camera_rules: Camera remains outside the airplane wing, no sudden cuts, smooth continuous movements, consistent lighting and color palette across scenes
style_bible.lighting_rules: Soft natural daylight with slight warm tones, directional light from above and front left, subtle shadows to enhance depth
style_bible.continuity_rules: Primary subject and environment remain constant; airflow visualization style and color coding consistent; camera movements flow naturally between scenes

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show a commercial airplane wing flying steadily through clear sky with airflow splitting above and below the wing.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY (state carried across scenes):
- primary subject: commercial airplane wing
- environment: clear blue sky with distant soft clouds
- explanatory state:
  - Scene 1: baseline condition (observation)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: wide shot, wing centered horizontally with leading edge on left
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly forward along wing span
- subject & action: smooth airflow streams approach wing from left, splitting into upper and lower flows by 3s
- environment details: distant horizon line, soft white clouds below wing, gentle blue gradient sky
- lighting: soft directional sunlight from top left, warm color temp
- cinematic look: natural contrast, moderate depth of field focusing on wing and airflow

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: medium shot, wing mid-span with airflow streams clearly visible
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow pan following airflow direction
- subject & action: airflow smoothly divides, upper stream curves over curved top surface, lower stream follows flatter underside
- environment details: same sky and clouds, subtle motion blur on airflow particles
- lighting: consistent soft sunlight, slight rim light on wing edges
- cinematic look: enhanced clarity on airflow patterns, subtle particle glow

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: medium close-up on wing cross-section from side angle
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: airflow streams clearly separate with visible speed difference cues (particle density and spacing)
- environment details: minimal sky background, focus on wing and airflow
- lighting: soft directional light emphasizing wing curvature
- cinematic look: high detail, shallow depth of field on wing surface

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: slow dolly forward along wing span, then slow pan following airflow direction, ending with static close-up side angle
scenes.audio_cue: Here we see a commercial airplane wing cruising through clear sky. Notice how the airflow approaches and splits smoothly above and below the wing, setting the stage to understand how this shape influences air movement.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Reveal the cause of airflow speed differences above and below the wing by zooming closer to the wing surface.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY (state carried across scenes):
- primary subject: commercial airplane wing
- environment: clear blue sky with distant soft clouds
- explanatory state:
  - Scene 2: mechanism revealed (cause)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: close-up on upper wing surface curvature
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: slow tilt down along wing surface
- subject & action: airflow particles accelerate over curved top, spacing increases indicating higher speed
- environment details: blurred sky background, subtle particle trails
- lighting: directional sunlight highlighting wing curvature
- cinematic look: sharp focus on airflow speed cues, soft bokeh background

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: close-up on lower wing surface
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: slow pan along lower surface
- subject & action: airflow particles move slower, denser spacing, less curvature
- environment details: consistent sky blur
- lighting: soft fill light to reveal underside details
- cinematic look: clear visualization of slower airflow

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: split screen style side-by-side view of upper and lower airflow
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: static
- subject & action: animated arrows and color-coded pressure zones appear off-wing (semi-transparent overlays) showing lower pressure above and higher pressure below
- environment details: minimal sky background
- lighting: balanced to emphasize overlays
- cinematic look: clean and informative visualization

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: slow tilt down upper surface, slow pan lower surface, static split side-by-side view
scenes.audio_cue: The wing’s curved upper surface causes air to speed up, while the flatter underside slows airflow. This speed difference creates lower pressure above and higher pressure below the wing, generating lift.

scenes.prompt:
SCENE INTENT: Show the effect of pressure difference producing lift force on the wing.

GLOBAL CONTINUITY (state carried across scenes):
- primary subject: commercial airplane wing
- environment: clear blue sky with distant soft clouds
- explanatory state:
  - Scene 3: outcome explained (effect)

SHOT 1 (0–3s):
- framing: medium shot of entire wing from side angle
- lens: 50mm
- camera movement: slow crane upward revealing wing and airflow
- subject & action: animated force vectors appear, arrow pointing upward from wing surface representing lift
- environment details: blue sky, soft clouds below
- lighting: soft directional sunlight
- cinematic look: clear visualization of forces with subtle glow

SHOT 2 (3–7s):
- framing: close-up on wing cross-section
- lens: 85mm
- camera movement: slow pan along wing surface
- subject & action: airflow streams with particle speed differences visible, pressure zones highlighted, lift arrow steady
- environment details: minimal sky background
- lighting: focused light to emphasize airflow and force arrows
- cinematic look: high detail, informative

SHOT 3 (7–10s):
- framing: wide shot showing wing in flight with airflow and lift arrow
- lens: 35mm
- camera movement: slow dolly backward
- subject & action: wing steadily moves forward with visible lift force sustaining flight
- environment details: expansive sky and clouds
- lighting: warm natural light
- cinematic look: cinematic clarity and depth

AVOID: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.negative_prompt: text, logos, subtitles, watermarks, extra limbs, distorted faces, flicker, glitch, people, human, face, hands, cockpit
scenes.camera: slow crane upward, slow pan along wing, slow dolly backward wide shot
scenes.audio_cue: This pressure difference creates an upward lift force on the wing, allowing the airplane to stay aloft as it moves forward through the air.
